input.title: Using Mean to Summarize Donation Data
input.setup_prose: A development officer in the Faculty of Arts and Sciences is reviewing donation amounts from a recent fundraising campaign. They want to understand the 'typical' gift size to set realistic goals for next year's outreach.
worked_steps: Step 1: Inspect the donation histogram — because there are many small gifts and a few very large ones, the data is not perfectly symmetrical.
Step 2: Calculate the mean of all donation amounts — because the mean balances the total value of all gifts, it provides a mathematical center for the distribution.

Step 3: Compare the mean to individual data points — because the mean represents a single point of central tendency, it acts as a reliable benchmark to distinguish typical donors from major gift prospects.
input.interpretation: In this scenario, the 'mean' (the average) provides a single number that summarizes the typical donation size. By identifying this central tendency, the development officer can quickly communicate typical donor behavior to stakeholders, helping them understand what an 'average' contribution looks like across the donor pool, rather than focusing on the extreme outliers who might skew the perception.

chart.caption: Distribution of individual donation amounts from the recent FAS campaign.
chart.provenance: Office of Development and Alumni Relations, 2023 Internal Report.

input.title: Defining 'Typical' Advising Loads
input.setup_prose: You are reviewing quarterly data on how many hours your staff spends in direct undergraduate advising. You need to report a single figure that represents the 'typical' advising load for your department's upcoming budget planning meeting.
input.artifact_summary_stats: Total staff surveyed: 57. Median: 14 hours/week. Mean: 13.8 hours/week.
input.observation_prompt: Before answering, take a moment: what do you notice about the distribution of hours?
observation_options: The values are clustered around the center.
The distribution is skewed to one side.
input.prompt: As an advising administrator looking at this distribution, which metric of central tendency would you use to describe the 'typical' weekly advising load to your colleagues, and why?

options.text: The mean (13.8 hours) best represents the 'typical' week for this office.
options.feedback: Selecting the mean is common because it represents the arithmetic average of all hours, but this interpretation overlooks how the distribution's shape might be influenced by a few staff members with very high or low advising loads.

options.text: The median (14 hours) is the best indicator of the 'typical' week because it identifies the middle point of our staff efforts.
options.feedback: Because this distribution is relatively balanced and centered, the median (14 hours) serves as a robust indicator of the middle, effectively splitting the staff group into two equal halves regardless of the specific range.

options.text: The mode is the most reliable metric because it identifies the highest peak in the chart.
options.feedback: One might choose this if they look only at the tallest bar (11-15 hours), assuming the most frequent experience equates to the 'typical' experience, even if it ignores the full scope of the dataset.

options.text: The range (0-30 hours) is the most accurate way to describe our standard advising capacity.
options.feedback: This assumes that the range itself is the primary indicator of typicality, though it only tells us about the extremes rather than where the majority of our staff's time is actually spent.

input.hint: Focus on the peak of the bars. Does the most frequent value (mode) align with the middle point (median) of the distribution?

chart.caption: Distribution of weekly hours spent on undergraduate advising sessions by staff members in the Dean's office.
chart.provenance: Office of Undergraduate Education, Internal Systems Audit 2023.

viz_decision.reason: rule-5: previous turn already used chart → vary or omit

input.title: Interpreting Course Feedback
input.setup_prose: You are an Academic Coordinator reviewing student feedback for a required undergraduate course. You have a table showing the distribution of numerical scores (1-5 scale) across all sections of the course, and you need to determine the 'typical' student experience to present at a faculty meeting.
input.prompt: Looking at this summary report of course evaluation scores from your department, how would you interpret the 'typical' student experience? Please structure your response by identifying your conclusion, providing the supporting evidence, and framing one follow-up question.
consider_scaffolds: What is the central tendency of the student evaluation scores?
Which specific data points lead you to identify that as the central tendency?
What additional information about the distribution would help you understand if the central tendency is a fair representation of the class experience?
input.rubric_primary_criterion: The response should demonstrate an ability to select an appropriate measure of central tendency and connect that choice to the specific characteristics of the data provided.

viz_decision.reason: rule-3: constructive tier → learner constructs interpretation, no visual crutch

input.title: Evaluating Typical Class Size
input.setup_prose: You are reviewing enrollment data for a set of undergraduate seminars and large lecture courses within your department. While the mean class size is often used for high-level reporting, some faculty members are concerned that this figure doesn't accurately reflect the experience of the average student. You need to provide a recommendation on which measure best captures the 'typical' enrollment.
input.prompt: Considering the distribution of course enrollment sizes across your department, how would you decide whether the mean or the median provides a more accurate representation of the 'typical' class size? Support your argument by explaining how specific features of your department's enrollment data might influence this choice.
input.rubric_primary_criterion: The learner demonstrates logical coherence by connecting the concept of central tendency (mean vs. median) to the shape of data distributions (e.g., presence of outliers or skewness).

viz_decision.reason: rule-2: WikiDraft tier → contribution-routine flowchart (process, not content)

input.title: Contributing to our Data Dictionary: Central Tendency
input.framing_prose: You have demonstrated a high level of data fluency regarding measures of central tendency. We would like to invite you to contribute your expertise to our cross-school dictionary. Your draft will be reviewed by our editorial team to ensure cross-school clarity before inclusion.
input.draft_template_hint: 1. Definition in your own words. 2. A Harvard-specific use case. 3. Common pitfalls in interpretation.

flowchart.provenance: Harvard Data Fluency Editorial Board Protocols

nodes.label: Define Concept
nodes.annotation: Propose a new term or update.

flowchart.caption: The collaborative editorial workflow for our institutional data dictionary.
